# Feedback Loop Latency

## The Physics

The time elapsed between performing an action and observing its consequence is
the single most important variable in system governability. When feedback is
fast — seconds to minutes — operators can correlate cause and effect, correct
errors before they compound, and build accurate mental models of system
behavior. When feedback is slow — hours, days, or weeks — the causal link
between action and outcome becomes ambiguous, errors accumulate undetected,
and governance degrades to guesswork.

This is control theory applied to engineering systems. A thermostat with a
one-second feedback loop holds temperature within a narrow band. The same
thermostat with a one-hour feedback loop produces wild oscillations. The
thermostat has not changed; the latency has.

Feedback latency is not merely an inconvenience — it determines the upper
bound on how precisely a system can be governed. No amount of process
discipline can compensate for feedback that arrives too late to be actionable.
Conversely, fast feedback can partially compensate for imperfect process,
because errors are caught and corrected before they propagate.

## Why This Matters

- **Testing**: The speed of the test suite determines how quickly an engineer learns whether a change is correct.
- **Deployment**: Time-to-detection for production issues determines blast radius.
- **Governance**: Review cycles that take weeks create governance debt — decisions are made in the gap.
- **Learning**: Engineers improve fastest when they see the consequences of their choices quickly.

## Observable Patterns

- Teams with fast CI pipelines (< 10 minutes) catch integration errors before they reach review.
- Production incidents correlate with observability gaps — where feedback latency is highest, incident duration is longest.
- Manual approval gates that add days of latency often increase risk rather than reducing it, because they decouple action from context.
- The most effective monitoring systems are those that surface anomalies in near-real-time, not those that produce the most comprehensive weekly reports.

## 1970/2070 Note

A 1970 mainframe programmer submitting a batch job waited hours for output on
a line printer — and structured work around that latency by desk-checking code
before submission. A 2070 quantum systems engineer will need real-time
decoherence telemetry to maintain qubit fidelity during computation. The
universal truth holds: shorten the loop, improve the governance.

## Relationship to Meta Layer

- JOS-P15 (Observable Systems) — observability is the infrastructure that makes fast feedback possible
- JOS-P16 (Automated Verification) — automation compresses feedback latency from human-speed to machine-speed
